title: READ_TRACKER LRU eviction does not actually implement LRU semantics (insertion-order only)
description:
## Severity
Trivial

## Dimension
Naming / Documentation & Intent

## Location
src/tools/file/mod.rs:29-55 (ReadFileTracker — comment says LRU-ordered)

## Violation
```rust
pub struct ReadFileTracker {
    /// LRU-ordered list: most recently read files at the end.
    /// When capacity is exceeded, oldest entries (front) are evicted.
    read_files: Mutex<Vec<PathBuf>>,
}

pub(crate) fn mark_read(&self, path: &Path) {
    ...
    files.retain(|p| p != &resolved);
    files.push(resolved);
    ...
}
```

Calling `mark_read(p)` after `has_been_read(p)` returns true does promote `p` to most-recent. But `has_been_read(p)` itself does NOT promote — it only reads. So a file that is *frequently checked* but never *re-marked* will be evicted while a file that was marked once 10k entries ago and never touched again gets kept (until something else pushes 10k more entries). The doc says LRU; the implementation is "insertion order, MRU on re-insert." These are different policies.

The distinction matters because edit_file calls has_been_read(); a session that does many edits but few reads can have its frequently-edited file evicted while a never-touched file blocks the slot.

## Mandated refactor
Either:
1. Make the policy match the docs: have has_been_read also promote (turn it into &mut self and acquire the write lock — currently it only takes the lock; turn it from `contains` to a remove-then-push-at-end).
2. OR fix the docs to say "insertion order with most-recent-touch promotion on mark_read" and explicitly document that has_been_read does not promote.

Cross-references issue #787 (READ_TRACKER global mutex) — the right fix is to use a real LRU container (linked_hash_map) so the policy is mechanical.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
